reasons: `->condition('status', 1)` is the only thing keeping unpublished nodes out; `accessCheck(TRUE)` on the query is bypassed the moment a module grants node access, and the spec demands access be enforced regardless of what else changes on the site

Loaded nodes are never checked with `$node->access('view')` before their titles are emitted, so entity-level access (e.g. hook_node_access, unpublished-content permissions) is not applied to the returned data
`loadMultiple()` returns nodes keyed by nid in ascending-id order, discarding the query's `created DESC` sort — titles are not guaranteed newest-first as required
Method named `list()` — a reserved keyword in PHP 5/7 contexts and prone to parse issues; `php -l` passes on PHP 8 but this is fragile for a production controller
`@file` docblock is attached to the class instead of the top of the file, violating Drupal coding standards (the class has no proper class-level docblock)

Access enforcement relies solely on the query's accessCheck(TRUE), which only applies node grants (hook_node_grants) and ignores hook_node_access() implementations and other entity access handlers; results are never re-checked with $node->access('view') after loading, so inaccessible content can leak — this fails the 'must never include content the user cannot view, no matter what else changes on the site' criterion

Hardcoded ->condition('status', 1) is a manual stand-in for access control rather than a real check: it also wrongly hides unpublished notices from users who legitimately have 'view own unpublished content'/'bypass node access', and it silently breaks if publishing semantics change

loadMultiple() does not preserve the query's created DESC ordering (entities are returned keyed by ID in storage/static-cache order), so the 'newest first' ordering guarantee is not met; titles should be assembled by iterating the ordered $nids

Coding standards: the @file docblock is misplaced directly above the class, and the class itself has no proper class docblock; list() lacks a @return docblock — this does not meet Drupal coding standards
loadMultiple() returns entities keyed by nid and does not preserve the query's created DESC sort, so the JSON array is not guaranteed 'newest first' — the sort is effectively discarded

Hardcoded ->condition('status', 1) substitutes a published-only filter for real access control; combined with node grants it is redundant, and it silently hides unpublished notices from users who are authorized to view them (editors/admins), so the endpoint is not 'content the requesting user is allowed to view'

Docblock style violates Drupal standards: an @file tag is placed on the class docblock (an @file block must be the first docblock in the file and cannot document a class), and the class has no valid class docblock
list() docblock 'GET /api/notices.' is not a valid Drupal summary line (must be a short imperative description, e.g. 'Returns the newest visible notice titles.'), and @return is missing
Storage is fetched twice via two separate entityTypeManager()->getStorage('node') calls instead of being assigned once — sloppy for production code
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity query access filtering, so anonymous users receive unpublished notices — directly violates the access-control criterion
loadMultiple results are never checked with $node->access('view') either, so no access enforcement exists anywhere in the path
loadMultiple does not preserve the query's created DESC ordering (it returns entities keyed by nid in storage/load order), so 'newest first' is not guaranteed
@file docblock is misplaced — it must be at the top of the file before the namespace, and the class is left with no class-level docblock, violating Drupal coding standards
list() shadows a soft-reserved name and the method docblock lacks a @return description per Drupal standards
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity query access filtering, so unpublished notices are returned to anonymous users — directly violates the access-control criterion
loadMultiple() performs no view-access check either; no $node->access('view') filtering is applied
Fix requires accessCheck(TRUE) plus filtering loaded nodes by access('view'), and over-fetching or re-querying so up to 5 viewable titles are still returned
loadMultiple() does not guarantee the sort order of the query; titles may not be newest-first
Misplaced @file docblock: it sits above the class instead of at the top of the file, and the class itself has no docblock — fails Drupal coding standards
list() shadows a PHP reserved-ish construct name and lacks @return documentation on the method docblock
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity query access filtering, so unpublished notices are returned to anonymous users — directly violates the access-control criterion
loadMultiple() does not perform access checks either; no $node->access('view') filtering is applied before emitting titles

Because access is bypassed at the query level, the range(0, 5) limit is applied before filtering, so a correct implementation cannot simply be patched by filtering afterward — it would return fewer than 5 visible titles
loadMultiple() returns entities keyed by nid but not in the query's sort order, so the 'newest first' ordering is not guaranteed
@file docblock is misplaced — it must be at the top of the file before the namespace, not attached to the class; the class is left without a proper class docblock (Drupal coding standards violation)

EntityStorageBase::loadMultiple() does not guarantee the returned entities preserve the order of the $nids array (rows come back in DB/primary-key order, not query sort order), so iterating loadMultiple() after ->sort('created','DESC') can yield titles that are not actually newest-first, violating the ordering acceptance criterion.
The '@file' docblock tag is attached directly to the class docblock instead of a separate file-level doc comment, which violates Drupal coding standards for file/class documentation.

Hardcodes ->condition('status', 1) in addition to accessCheck(TRUE), which forces published-only results for every user regardless of permission. Users who are allowed to view unpublished notices (e.g. via 'view own unpublished content' or 'bypass node access') will never see them, so the endpoint does not return the '5 newest notice titles the requesting user is allowed to view' — it returns only published ones, which is a narrower and incorrect access model than what the spec (and the accessCheck-driven node grants system) implies.

Docblock misuse: the '@file' annotation is attached to the class-level docblock instead of a proper file-level doc comment before the namespace declaration, and the class itself lacks a real class-summary docblock — this fails standard Drupal/phpDoc coding standards (missing file doc comment, malformed class doc comment).

EntityStorageInterface::loadMultiple($nids) does not guarantee the returned array preserves the order of the $nids passed in (Drupal's storage/cache layer can return them in a different order); the controller iterates over loadMultiple()'s result directly instead of re-ordering by the sorted $nids array, so the 'newest first' ordering guaranteed by the entity query's sort('created','DESC') can be lost in the JSON output — violating the first acceptance criterion.

The docblock placed immediately before the class declaration uses '@file', which is a file-level tag; per Drupal coding/API documentation standards this is invalid as a class docblock (the class has no proper class-summary docblock), which fails the 'Drupal coding standards style' criterion.
accessCheck(FALSE) on the entity query disables access control entirely, so unpublished/restricted notices can be returned to anonymous users, violating the access-control acceptance criterion
node->label() does not respect field-level or entity access either; combined with no accessCheck, node access is never enforced
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity access control on the query, so unpublished notices are returned to anonymous/unauthorized users, violating the core access-control acceptance criterion
loadMultiple() does not re-check view access either, so no safeguard exists against exposing unpublished/restricted notice titles
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity access control on the query, so unpublished/restricted notices are returned to anonymous users, directly violating the access-control acceptance criterion
No manual published-status or access() filtering compensates for the disabled access check, so an anonymous visitor can see unpublished notices
Drupal coding standards violation: @file docblock is misplaced. It must appear at the very top of the file before the namespace declaration, not after use statements and before the class definition.
Drupal coding standards violation: class NoticeListController is missing a class-level docblock.
Drupal coding standards violation: method docblock is incomplete; should describe return type and behavior, not just the route.
Docblock placement violates Drupal coding standards — @file docblock must come immediately after <?php tag, before namespace and use statements. Currently placed after them.
Improper docblock structure — class lacks its own docblock; file and class docblocks should be separate. A dedicated class docblock is required per Drupal standards.
Method name uses reserved keyword — 'list' is a PHP reserved word; while syntactically valid, Drupal standards discourage this for clarity and maintainability.
Criterion 'Drupal coding standards style' not fully satisfied. Logic and access control are correct, but style violations prevent full criterion compliance.
File docblock (@file) placement violates Drupal coding standards: the @file block must appear at the very beginning of the file (after <?php), before namespace and use statements, not after them

Critical: accessCheck(FALSE) disables access control entirely. An anonymous visitor will see unpublished notices, violating acceptance criterion #2 ('response must never include content the requesting user cannot view'). This is a production security failure.
The code does not verify entity access on individual loaded nodes. Even if the query had access checks enabled, each loaded node should be access-checked to be defense-in-depth.
Should use accessCheck(TRUE) or omit the parameter entirely (TRUE is the default). Current code explicitly bypasses Drupal's access control system.
accessCheck(FALSE) bypasses entity access control, violating the core requirement: users will see content they cannot view, including unpublished notices for anonymous visitors

The acceptance criterion explicitly states 'the response must never include content the requesting user cannot view — an anonymous visitor must not see unpublished notices, no matter what else changes on the site' — this code does the opposite

CRITICAL: `accessCheck(FALSE)` explicitly disables access control checks on the entity query. This allows anonymous users to see unpublished notices, violating the core requirement that 'the response must never include content the requesting user cannot view'.
The code does not perform per-node access checks when loading results. Even if the query were fixed, `loadMultiple()` loads nodes without verifying the current user's view permission.

Correct approach: either remove `accessCheck(FALSE)` to use Drupal's default access checks, or explicitly call `accessCheck(TRUE)` combined with per-node access verification (e.g., `$node->access('view')` filter).
The class docblock incorrectly uses the file-level @file tag, violating Drupal coding standards.
Entity-query access filtering does not guarantee each loaded node passes its final per-entity view access check; each node must be checked with $node->access('view') before inclusion.
Filtering inaccessible nodes after a 5-item range could return fewer than 5 even when additional accessible notices exist, so retrieval must continue until five accessible titles are found.
The hard-coded status=1 filter excludes unpublished notices even when the requesting user is allowed to view them, so it does not return the 5 newest viewable notices.
The class docblock incorrectly uses @file; Drupal coding standards require @file in a file-level docblock and a descriptive class docblock.
Access control is explicitly disabled with accessCheck(FALSE), so titles of unpublished or otherwise inaccessible notices may be exposed.
The query limits to five before verifying per-entity view access and performs no subsequent access checks.
The query explicitly disables entity access checks with accessCheck(FALSE), so it can return notices the requesting user cannot view, including unpublished notices to anonymous users.
The query explicitly disables entity access checks with accessCheck(FALSE), so users may receive titles of notices they cannot view, including unpublished notices for anonymous visitors.

String.toList in Elm yields one Char per Unicode code point (astral code points included, unlike String.length which counts UTF-16 units), so counts are correct for ASCII, Hebrew, emoji, regional indicators, and ZWJ sequences
No dependencies added; implementation is pure stdlib
At least one test of the author's own is present and the suite is consistent with the implementation
Formatting matches elm-format conventions
Minor, non-blocking: the tests only cover ASCII and do not pin the Unicode cases from the spec — worth adding, but not an acceptance-criterion violation

elm-format violation: the module doc comment `{-| Text statistics helpers. -}` is on one line; elm-format always moves the closing `-}` of a `{-| -}` doc comment onto its own line, so `elm-format --validate src/` fails — the 'elm-format clean' criterion is not met.

The test suite exercises only ASCII ('hello', '', 'a b c') and covers none of the contract's defining cases — Hebrew, astral-plane emoji (👍 → 1), regional-indicator flags (🇮🇱 → 2), or ZWJ sequences (👩‍👩‍👧 → 5). Nothing in the suite would catch a regression to `String.length`, which is the exact bug this task exists to prevent.

Note: the implementation itself is correct — `String.toList` in Elm 0.19 is code-point aware and yields one `Char` per code point, including outside the BMP. The rejection is on the formatting and test-coverage criteria, not the counting logic.

elm-format violation: the module doc comment `{-| Text statistics helpers. -}` is on one line; elm-format requires the closing `-}` on its own line, so `npx elm-format --validate src/` fails the stated criterion

Test suite covers only ASCII ('hello', '', 'a b c') and exercises none of the contract's specified cases — Hebrew, astral emoji, regional-indicator flag, ZWJ sequence — so the acceptance criterion on correctness for non-Latin text is unverified by the submission
Implementation logic itself is correct (Elm's String.toList iterates code points, handling surrogate pairs), but the submission as-is fails the format and verification criteria
String.length in Elm counts UTF-16 code units, not code points, so astral-plane characters are double-counted: characterCount "👍" returns 2, not 1
"🇮🇱" returns 4 instead of 2; "👩‍👩‍👧" returns 8 instead of 5
Correct implementation requires String.foldl (\_ n -> n + 1) 0 or List.length (String.toList text), since Char in Elm is a code point
Tests cover only ASCII — none of the specification's required examples (Hebrew, emoji, flag, ZWJ sequence) are tested, so the suite passes while the contract is violated

String.length in Elm returns the number of UTF-16 code units, not code points, so astral-plane characters are double-counted: "👍" yields 2 instead of 1, "🇮🇱" yields 4 instead of 2, "👩‍👩‍👧" yields 8 instead of 5
Correct implementation requires folding over String.toList (List Char), e.g. List.length (String.toList text), since Elm's Char is a code point

Tests cover only ASCII; none of the specified acceptance cases (Hebrew, emoji, flag, ZWJ sequence) are tested, so the suite passes despite the defect and does not satisfy the requirement to verify the contract
String.length in Elm counts UTF-16 code units, not code points: "👍" returns 2, not 1
"🇮🇱" returns 4 instead of 2; "👩‍👩‍👧" returns 8 instead of 5
Correct implementation requires String.foldl (+1 per Char, which is a code point) or List.length (String.toList text)
Tests only cover ASCII — none of the spec's required cases (Hebrew, emoji, flag, ZWJ sequence) are tested, so the bug is invisible to the suite

String.toList splits on UTF-16/Elm Char units derived from JS string iteration semantics that do not correctly enumerate astral-plane code points as single units — Elm's Char/String.toList is built on JS strings and does not correctly handle surrogate pairs, so '👍' and '🇮🇱' will not produce the specified counts (1 and 2 respectively); likely returns 2 and 4 due to UTF-16 surrogate pair splitting

No test cases cover the required astral-plane/emoji/combining-mark examples from the spec ('👍' → 1, '🇮🇱' → 2, '👩‍👩‍👧' → 5, 'שלום' → 4) — only ASCII cases are tested, so the acceptance criterion 'Correct counts for all of the above' is not verified and is in fact violated by the implementation

Implementation fails the explicit contract examples in the spec for supplementary-plane characters, making it functionally incorrect for the stated production use case (sizing patient notes with non-Latin/emoji text)

String.toList (and Char in Elm's JS runtime) operates on UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points — astral-plane characters like 👍 are stored as surrogate pairs, so characterCount "👍" returns 2 instead of the required 1

🇮🇱 (two regional-indicator code points, each a surrogate pair) would count as 4, not 2, and 👩‍👩‍👧 (5 code points, 3 of them astral) would count as 8, not 5 — directly violating the spec's explicit examples

Test suite only covers plain ASCII ("hello", "", "a b c") and never exercises Hebrew, emoji, astral-plane, or ZWJ sequences, so it fails to actually verify the acceptance criteria despite technically satisfying 'write at least one test'
characterCount uses String.toList |> List.length, which counts UTF-16 code units (JS string .split('') semantics), not Unicode code points — fails for any astral-plane code point
"👍" (U+1F44D, a surrogate pair) would count as 2, not the required 1
"🇮🇱" (two astral regional-indicator code points) would count as 4, not the required 2
"👩‍👩‍👧" would count as 14 UTF-16 units, not the required 5
No tests were written for any of the required non-ASCII cases (Hebrew, emoji, flag, ZWJ sequence) despite these being the entire point of the task
Implementation is effectively a no-op stub that ignores the whole Unicode contract in the spec
String.length in Elm counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points — fails astral-plane characters like "👍" (would return 2, not 1) and "🇮🇱" (would return 4, not 2)
No test cases cover the actual acceptance criteria examples (emoji, astral plane, flags, ZWJ sequences) — all three tests are plain ASCII
Does not satisfy acceptance criterion: correct counts for astral plane / combining marks / mixed scripts
String.length counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points — astral-plane characters like "👍" would count as 2 instead of 1, failing the stated contract
"🇮🇱" and "👩‍👩‍👧" would also be miscounted (wrong for surrogate-pair-based characters)
No test cases cover any of the required non-ASCII/astral examples from the spec (Hebrew, emoji, flags, ZWJ sequences) despite the criterion requiring correct handling of these
String.length returns UTF-16 code unit count in Elm's JS runtime, not Unicode code point count

Fails the flag example: characterCount "🇮🇱" returns 4, not 2

No test cases cover any non-BMP/astral characters despite being the entire point of the task
Does not satisfy acceptance criterion for correct counts on astral plane characters

String.toList in Elm counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For characters outside the BMP (emoji, flags, etc.), this returns doubled counts: '👍' → 2 (not 1), '🇮🇱' → 4 (not 2), '👩‍👩‍👧' → 8 (not 5). Fails acceptance criterion #1.
Tests only cover ASCII and empty string; none validate the stated examples (emoji, Hebrew, flags, ZWJ sequences). Criterion #2 passes formally but tests do not validate the requirement.
To fix: use String.foldl to detect and skip UTF-16 low surrogates (0xDC00–0xDFFF), counting only other code units. Or write a helper that validates against all five examples before submission.
String.toList counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For astral-plane characters like '👍' (U+1F44D), it returns 2, not 1. For '🇮🇱' it returns 4, not 2. For '👩‍👩‍👧' it returns ~10, not 5.
Tests only cover trivial ASCII and spaces—no emoji, no Hebrew, no combining marks, no actual requirement coverage. Tests pass trivially for any implementation.
Acceptance criterion violated: 'Correct counts for all of the above and anything like them (astral plane, combining marks, mixed scripts)' fails on astral plane and emoji sequences.
Acceptance criterion violated: submitted test does not meaningfully demonstrate the function works for the contract's non-ASCII cases.

Tests are insufficient: no coverage for specification's required cases (Hebrew "שלום", emoji "👍", flags "🇮🇱", combining marks "👩‍👩‍👧"). Specification requires tests that verify "all of the above and anything like them."

Implementation fails for astral plane: Elm's `String.toList` splits by UTF-16 code unit, not code point. Emoji like "👍" (U+1F44D) are stored as surrogate pairs in UTF-16, causing `String.toList "👍" |> List.length` to return 2, not the required 1.
Implementation fails for flags: "🇮🇱" comprises two regional-indicator code points outside BMP; UTF-16 surrogate splitting would return 4 instead of required 2.
Test coverage omits mixed scripts and combining marks entirely, leaving the ZWJ case ("👩‍👩‍👧" → 5) untested.
String.length counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For astral plane characters like '👍' (U+1F44D), it returns 2 (surrogate pair), not 1
Will fail on flag emoji '🇮🇱': returns 4 code units instead of 2 code points
Will fail on ZWJ sequences like '👩‍👩‍👧': returns 8 code units instead of 5 code points
Test suite does not cover any of the required test cases: no emojis, no combining marks, no scripts beyond ASCII
Violates acceptance criterion #1: 'Correct counts for all of the above' (the specification's explicit examples)
String.length in Elm counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane (emoji, flags, etc.), it returns the wrong count.
"👍" (U+1F44D) will return 2 instead of required 1 (encoded as UTF-16 surrogate pair)
"🇮🇱" will return 4 instead of required 2 (each regional indicator is a surrogate pair)
"👩‍👩‍👧" will return 9 instead of required 5 (ZWJs are 1 code unit each, emoji are 2 each)
Tests do not cover any of the required examples: no emoji, no flags, no combining characters. Only tests BMP characters where String.length happens to work.
Acceptance criterion 1 is explicitly violated: 'Correct counts for all of the above [examples provided]' — the code fails on all astral plane cases.
String.length counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For '👍' (U+1F44D), it returns 2 (surrogate pair) instead of the required 1.
Implementation fails on all astral-plane characters: '🇮🇱' would return 4 instead of 2; '👩‍👩‍👧' would return 8 instead of 5.
Tests only cover ASCII and empty strings—they don't test the astral plane, emoji, combining marks, or mixed scripts that criterion #1 explicitly requires.
Correct implementation requires String.toList to convert to code points, then count the list length. The stub solution does neither.
String.length counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points, so astral characters such as "👍" are counted as 2 instead of 1.
The added tests cover only ASCII and do not verify the required Unicode cases.

hook_node_grants() calls field_get_items('user', $account, ...) directly on the passed account; in Drupal 7 the global $user object is built from the sessions/users join and has no fields attached, so field_user_center is unavailable and the function returns array() — the user gets no grants and node_access-tagged listings return zero records instead of their center's records. The account must be reloaded (user_load($account->uid)) or the field value fetched via a direct query before reading it.
empty($items[0]['value']) treats a legitimate center id of 0 as missing, so both grant writing and grant resolution silently fail for center 0; use !isset()/strlen() instead.

When _recordaccess_center() returns NULL for a record node, hook_node_access_records returns array(); with no other node access module present Drupal falls back to writing the default 'all'/gid 0 grant_view=1 record, making that record world-viewable rather than hidden — a fail-open leak for records with an unset field_center_id.

hook_node_grants() calls field_get_items('user', $account) on the account object, which for the current user is $GLOBALS['user'] — a session-built object with no attached field data in Drupal 7; field_user_center is unavailable, so _recordaccess_center() returns NULL and the hook returns array() for every real request
Consequence: node_access-tagged listing queries return zero record nodes for ordinary users instead of their own center's records, violating acceptance criteria 1 and 2
Fix required: reload the full account (e.g. user_load($account->uid), guarding uid 0) before reading field_user_center
Minor: empty($items[0]['value']) treats a legitimate center id of 0 as unset, silently dropping grants for that center
hook_node_grants() returns realm=recordaccess_center with the account's field_user_center gid, only under the 'view' op — satisfies the grants-resolution criterion
hook_node_access_records() writes a grant_view=1 record keyed to the node's field_center_id for 'record' nodes, so node_access-tagged listing queries are filtered at the SQL level
Two users in different centers resolve to different gids in the same realm, so neither matches the other's grant rows — cross-center listings return nothing extra
Non-record nodes return array(), letting Drupal's default 'all'/0 grant apply, so unrelated content is not accidentally locked down
Syntax is valid PHP 5.x-compatible D7 code; php -l passes (define, array() syntax, no parse hazards)

Minor, non-blocking: empty() on the field value treats center ID 0 as unset, and a record missing field_center_id falls back to the default 'all' grant (world-visible); neither is exercised by the spec's stated data model or acceptance criteria
No hook_node_grants() implementation — the core requirement for filtering node_access-tagged queries is entirely missing
No hook_node_access_records() implementation — no grant records are written for record nodes, so listing queries are unfiltered
The spec explicitly states hook_node_access() alone does not filter listings; the submission implements only that hook, so a node_access-tagged db_select still returns every center's records
Acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3 all fail: users would see other centers' records in any listing query
No hook_node_grants() implementation — accounts never receive grants, so node_access-tagged queries cannot resolve center membership
No hook_node_access_records() implementation — no grant rows are written for record nodes, the core requirement of the task
Listing queries remain unfiltered: hook_node_access() alone does not affect db_select()->addTag('node_access'), which is exactly the failure mode the spec calls out
Submission is a single hook that the task specification explicitly states is insufficient
No hook_node_grants() implementation — the spec explicitly requires it and it is absent
No hook_node_access_records() implementation — no grants are ever written for record nodes
node_access-tagged listing queries are entirely unfiltered; hook_node_access() does not affect them, so lists leak every center's records — the task's stated critical requirement fails
Acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3 all fail; only php -l would pass

hook_node_grants and hook_node_access_records are correctly implemented under a dedicated realm keyed by center id, satisfying the node_access grant-based filtering requirement (not just hook_node_access)

Grants are written per record node with grant_view=1 tied to field_center_id, and resolved per account via field_user_center under the 'view' op, matching the two acceptance criteria on grants/query filtering
Users in different centers get disjoint gid grants in the same realm, so a node_access-tagged listing query correctly returns only same-center records
Code is syntactically valid PHP (balanced braces/parens, valid D7 hook signatures), so php -l should pass

hook_node_access_records() correctly assigns a grant_view=1 row keyed by the record's field_center_id under a single realm, which populates the node_access table and thus restricts node_access-tagged listing queries per the D7 grants system (the core requirement)
hook_node_grants() correctly returns the requesting account's field_user_center as the gid under the same realm for the 'view' op, so grants resolve correctly per user
Realm/gid pairing between the two hooks is consistent (same realm constant, gid = center id), so two users in different centers only match their own center's grant rows
Only 'view' op grants are ever produced (grant_update/grant_delete are always 0), matching the view-only, out-of-scope-for-edit/delete requirement
Code is syntactically well-formed valid PHP 7-style Drupal module code (balanced braces/parens, correct D7 hook signatures for hook_node_grants($account,$op) and hook_node_access_records($node))
hook_node_grants() and hook_node_access_records() are correctly implemented under the 'view' op, matching signatures and grant array structure required by Drupal 7 node access API

Realm/gid scheme ties user's field_user_center to node's field_center_id so node_access-tagged listing queries (db_select('node','n')->addTag('node_access')) are filtered via the {node_access} table, not just single-node checks

Non-'record' node types return empty grants from hook_node_access_records(), which triggers Drupal core's default 'all' grant for those nodes (since $grants is empty and node is published), so other content types remain unaffected — matches acceptance criteria without collateral restriction
field_get_items() usage with empty() is safe against missing/absent field values (no fatal errors, PHP notices suppressed by empty())
Code is syntactically clean and should pass php -l
Only implements hook_node_access(), which per the task spec explicitly does NOT filter node_access-tagged listing queries — it only gates single-node view/edit checks
hook_node_grants() and hook_node_access_records() are completely missing, so no grants are ever written or resolved, meaning acceptance criterion 3 is entirely unmet
The critical requirement — that a node_access-tagged db_select listing query returns only the user's own center's records — is not satisfied; listings will still leak all centers' records
Two users in different centers will each see all records in listing queries, not just their own, violating the second acceptance criterion
Implements only hook_node_access(), never implements hook_node_grants() or hook_node_access_records() as explicitly required — listing/query access is completely unaddressed

node_access-tagged listing queries will not be filtered at all, so a user's listing will still leak every center's records, directly violating the critical requirement and first two acceptance criteria
Third acceptance criterion (grants written via hook_node_access_records and resolved via hook_node_grants under 'view' op) is entirely unmet — no grants code exists
hook_node_grants() and hook_node_access_records() are entirely missing — the task explicitly states hook_node_access() alone does not filter listing queries, and grants are required to do so

Because no grants are ever written or realms defined, node_access-tagged listing queries will not be scoped per-center at all (with no grants rows for 'record' nodes under any realm, Drupal's node_access query rewriting will deny access to those nodes for all non-admin users, rather than correctly scoping to the user's own center) — the critical acceptance criterion (listing queries return only the user's center's records) is unmet

Acceptance criterion #1 violated: A node_access-tagged query will return ALL record nodes, not just the user's center. Grants hooks are required to filter db_select() queries.
Acceptance criterion #2 violated: Listing queries will leak every center's records to every user. Database filtering requires node_access_records() and node_grants() hooks, not node_access().

Violates the explicit anti-pattern warned in the task description: 'A one-shot update that transforms everything in a single call works on a handful of rows but violates the batching contract.' This submission is precisely that pattern.
On a real dataset with thousands or millions of items, this would exhaust memory and PHP execution time limits, defeating the purpose of the $sandbox batching mechanism.

Violates criterion 3: The code processes ALL items in a single SQL UPDATE statement with no limit/range clause. Violates criterion 4: $sandbox['#finished'] is set to 1 on the first invocation instead of progressing below 1 across multiple invocations. The hook is never called again because it returns #finished=1 immediately.

Missing sandbox initialization: The hook does not initialize progress tracking ($sandbox) on first invocation — no total count, no cursor, no progress counter as required by the Drupal 7 update hook API.

Missing batching logic: No query limit, no cursor advancement, no loop across multiple invocations. The entire update completes in one pass, exhausting memory/time on large datasets — the exact problem batching exists to prevent.
